upostmessage

upostmessage

Post an asynchronous message to a Uniface component instance.

int UPostMessage (char*dnp, char*dest, char*msgId, char*msg);
Note: Deprecated. Use upostmes instead, which supports TLS, performs code page conversion, and works on all platforms, not just Windows.

Parameters

dnp
network connector string that identifies a Uniface Router and, partially, the target application:
{ HostId }{+PortNumber} |UserName

  • HostId—host name or IP address where the Uniface Router is running. If omitted, the default is localhost ( the current machine). The protocol used is always IPv4.

  • PortNumber—port number on which the Uniface Router is listening. If omitted, the default value is 13001.

  • UserName—user name under which the target application is running.

dest
Destination. The name of the target application and component:
SymbolicName:{InstanceName}

    • SymbolicName—application identifier of the target application under which it registered with the Uniface Router.

    • InstanceName—name of a component instance in that application.

msgId
Message identifier for the message. It must contain at least one non-blank character. Trailing blanks are ignored. The maximum length of msgId is given in the table below.
msg
Data of the message. The maximum length of msg is given in the table below.

For the maximum lengths of HostID, UserName, SymbolicName, and InstanceName, see the Size Limits section.

Return Values

Table 1. Values returned by upostmessage
Value Meaning
0 Success. The message was put in the message queue for the target instance.
-12001 Host identifier not specified
-12002 Host identifier too long (PM_MAXHOST)
-12003 User name not specified
-12004 User name too long (PM_MAXUSER)
-12005 Symbolic name not specified
-12006 Symbolic name too long (PM_MAXUST)
-12007 Instance name too long (PM_MAXINSTANCE)
-12009 Message ID too long (PM_MAXMSGDATA)
-12011 Message data too long (PM_MAXMSGDATA)
-12017 Initialize socket failed
-12018 Send string to the server failed
-12019 Receive TCP message failed

Use

Microsoft Windows only, in library upostmsg

Description

upostmessage sends an asynchronous message to a Uniface component instance. This function is provided only on Microsoft Windows platforms, although the target instance can be on any platform that supports receiving asynchronous messages. The target application must be registered with a local Uniface Router. For more information, see the "Asynchronous Messaging" topic.

The first parameter dnp is the path information that identifies the (local or remote) Uniface Router to send the message to. No password needs to be provided here.

The second parameter dest is passed to the Uniface Router so that it can look up the target application in its list of registered applications, and post the message to the correct process. If an instance name is provided, it posts the message to the receiveMessage trigger of that instance. If this instance cannot be found in the application, or if no instance name was provided, it posts the message to the receiveMessage trigger of the application. Note that the colon (:) is mandatory, even if no instance name is provided (see the example below).

Also note that successfully posting a message with upostmessage does not guarantee actual delivery of the message. The message will be put on a queue to be picked up by the targeted application or instance. Neither upostmessage or the Uniface Router can determine if and when that will happen.

Size Limits

The following size limits hold for the parameters and their individual parts:

Table 2. Size limits for postmessage parameters
Parameter description Literal name Limit
Host identifier PM_MAXHOST 128
User name PM_MAXUSER 128
Instance path PM_MAXUST 16
Instance name PM_MAXINSTANCE 16
MessageID PM_MAXMSGID 32
Message data PM_MAXMSGDATA 512
Port number PM_MAXPORTNR 8
DNP PM_MAXDNP 16

The following C program posts a message to the receiveMessage trigger of a remote application with UST foobar.

#include <stdio>
#include <h3gl.h>
main ()
{
upostmessage(
"host01+13001|fred", /* User 'fred' on host 'host01' */
"foobar:", /* Ust 'foobar' */
"Msg01", /* Message ID */
"I Am An Async Message" ); /* Message body */
}

On the machine host01, the Uniface Router must be running on port 13001. User fred must have started the application and registered it with the local Uniface Router as UST foobar, like this:

bin\uniface.exe /dnp=tcp:+13001 /ust=foobar appname

The receiveMessage trigger of this application will be fired, receiving the message ID and text in $msgid and $msgdata, respectively.

There is also an executable version of the function, umsgutil.exe, provided on MS Windows platforms in the Uniface bin directory. This program takes four command line arguments that correspond to the four function parameters. The example could be run from the command line as follows:

bin\umsgutil /dnp="host01+13001|fred" /dst="foobar:" /mid=Msg01 /msg="I Am An Async Message"Note the selective use of double quotes to prevent the command interpreter from interpreting and breaking up the parameters.For more details, refer to the MsgUtility example in the samples directory of your Uniface installation.
Related Links
  • Asynchronous Messaging

    To communicate with each other asynchronously, Uniface components can send asynchronous messages (often known as postmessages). Components can send messages to other components running in the same Uniface client process, in other client processes, and in shared Uniface Server processes. Messages sent between different processes are transported via the Uniface Router.

  • umsgutil.exe

    Command line interface for the Message API Utility.
